title: Attempting to mount volume on nfsv4 expects nfsv3 things and fails
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Attempting to mount volume on nfsv4 expects nfsv3 things and fails.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. podman volume create --opt type=nfs --opt o=rw --opt device=my.nfs.host:/nfs-fast/podman-storage ssdvoltest

2. podman volume inspect ssdvoltest
[
     {
          "Name": "ssdvoltest",
          "Driver": "local",
          "Mountpoint": "/home/ladm-dnaumov/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes/ssdvoltest/_data",
          "CreatedAt": "2022-10-27T10:49:09.990125571+03:00",
          "Labels": {},
          "Scope": "local",
          "Options": {
               "device": "my.nfs.host:/nfs-fast/podman-storage",
               "o": "rw",
               "type": "nfs"
          },
          "MountCount": 0,
          "NeedsCopyUp": true,
          "NeedsChown": true
     }
]

3. podman run -it -d -v ssdvoltest:/mnt/myfolder busybox

**Describe the results you received:**

Error: error mounting volume ssdvoltest for container 9f106d4d2e5fd1028e37c8a36cd5c2e1e524fbe2d608df263533411e5fcebbbc: /usr/sbin/start-statd: line 10: /run/rpc.statd.lock: Permission denied
mount.nfs: rpc.statd is not running but is required for remote locking.
mount.nfs: Either use '-o nolock' to keep locks local, or start statd.
mount.nfs: Operation not permitted

**Describe the results you expected:**

Expected the mount to succeed. 

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

I am using NFSv4 (v3 and v2 are disabled entirely server-side) and NFSv4 doesn't use statd.

**Output of `podman version`:**

comments.body: Do you run this rootless? I don't think the kernel allows rootless users to mount nfs.

> Do you run this rootless? I don't think the kernel allows rootless users to mount nfs.

Yes, this is rootless. In a regular "nfs in fstab" situation, it's entirely possible to use the "user" option to allow unpriviledged users to mount an NFS share.
That is because the `mount` command is setuid and therefore runs as root, podman is not setuid and just uses the mount syscall.
Could we fall back to exec of mount command?

We do not use the Mount syscall, we use the Mount command.

Can you try using type=nfs4 instead of just nfs?

On Thu, Oct 27, 2022 at 07:26 Daniel J Walsh ***@***.***>
wrote:

> Could we fall back to exec of mount command?
>

> […](#)
> On Thu, Oct 27, 2022 at 07:26 Daniel J Walsh ***@***.***> wrote: Could we fall back to exec of mount command? — Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub <[#16316 (comment)](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/16316#issuecomment-1293384547)>, or unsubscribe <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AB3AOCBMX7WCG6VRQXH5MKLWFJRIBANCNFSM6AAAAAARPZXGMU> . You are receiving this because you are subscribed to this thread.Message ID: ***@***.***>

Error: error mounting volume ssdvoltest for container 1944dd9b81c0407748cceaf8a74953923fbd77a1bd82fae79ac2c8654e7f7599: mount.nfs4: Operation not permitted

Manually mounting the NFS share as the very same unpriviledged user works fine.

What's the mount command you are using to mount the volume?

Under the hood, when Podman mounts a volume, we are basically doing the following:

`mount -t <type> -o <o> <device> <Podman-managed mountpoint>`

Where `type` is from `--opt type=`, `o` is from `--opt o=`, and `device` is from `--opt device=`. So if you had a command like:

`mount -t nfs4 -o ro,async my.nfs.server:/srv/nfs/Music /mount`

You would mount that share with:

`podman volume create --opt type=nfs4 --opt o=async --opt device=my.nfs.server:/srv/nfs/Music myVol`

This should work as rootless, though I admit I haven't tried it with NFS in the past.

Even if we use the command https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/16316#issuecomment-1293400911 is still valid, mount needs suid for such  it and the real uid 0 is not mapped.
Try mounting inside the namespace with `podman unshare`.

> What's the mount command you are using to mount the volume?

[ladm-dnaumov@sigma ~]$ cat /etc/fstab | grep nfs

lingen.systems.dance:/nfs-fast/podman-storage /mnt/nfs-podman-fast nfs4    user,_netdev                                    0     0
lingen.systems.dance:/nfs-slow/podman-storage /mnt/nfs-podman-slow nfs4    user,_netdev                                    0     0

[ladm-dnaumov@sigma ~]$ whoami
ladm-dnaumov

[ladm-dnaumov@sigma ~]$ mount /mnt/nfs-podman-fast

[ladm-dnaumov@sigma ~]$ df -h | grep nfs
lingen.systems.dance:/nfs-fast/podman-storage  256G  1.9G  255G   1% /mnt/nfs-podman-fast

> Even if we use the command [#16316 (comment)](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/16316#issuecomment-1293400911) is still valid, mount needs suid for such it and the real uid 0 is not mapped. Try mounting inside the namespace with `podman unshare`.

[ladm-dnaumov@sigma ~]$ podman unshare podman run -it -d -v ssdvoltest:/mnt/myfolder busybox; echo $?
Error: error mounting volume ssdvoltest for container 2eec728e79695f6e3005a2f0c791c720038074b0f7e9cbbc41911b490798fe20: mount.nfs4: Operation not permitted

126
Just run `podman unshare` this will give you a shell in the podman user namespace. Then you can try `mount` there.

> Just run `podman unshare` this will give you a shell in the podman user namespace. Then you can try `mount` there.

[ladm-dnaumov@sigma ~]$ podman unshare

[root@sigma ~]$ podman run -it -d -v ssdvoltest:/mnt/myfolder busybox
Error: error mounting volume ssdvoltest for container 9199ba8ef2e580df9c3ff7cf50b629c4bed0034e5f99e231d5ceea78321b41c1: mount.nfs4: Operation not permitted

So am I getting it right that the ONLY way to use rootless Podman with NFS4 for volume storage is to set the volume_path to a destination that is an already mounted NFS share?

The main reason this is problematic is that a user may have access to very different tiers of storage, both local and remote. As can be deduced from my examples here, I have an NFS share backed by SSDs as well as a share backed by spinners. As root, it seems possible to use both simultaneously. As rootless, due to inability to use mount.nfs4 from podman user context pigeonholes me into setting volume_path to an already mounted fast NFS share combined with bind mounts to the already mounted slow NFS share.

I’ve already opened a feature request for the capability to have more than 1 volume_path available to a single podman user, as that would allow me to keep rootless AND use different storage options with volumes.
I don't think that's an accurate statement - if a rootless `mount` command on said NFS share works, Podman should be able to mount it.

Also, I *STRONGLY* recommend against volume-back changes. That is not what that field is intended to do, and we are almost certainly not adding support for additional volume paths. A `type=bind` volume would be a much better solution.

> Also, I _STRONGLY_ recommend against volume-back changes. That is not what that field is intended to do, and we are almost certainly not adding support for additional volume paths. 

May I ask what is the reasoning behind forcefully confining named volume users to a singular path option and denying them the ability to place named volumes onto different classes of storages based on expected workload?

> A `type=bind` volume would be a much better solution.

It's a non-solution as bind mounts are not named volumes.

For your second question, named volumes can be backed by bind mounts via the same mechanism used for NFS and similar mounts. `podman volume create -o type=bind -o o=bind /path/for/bind myVol`

For your first question, the intent of named volumes is to provide internally-managed volumes that can be mounted into containers. It is not intended to provide multiple volume stores on specific disks or NFS shares or similar. That is more the domain of the local volume driver (which can mount filesystems) or a separate volume plugin.

> I don't think so, AFAIK it is impossible to run the suid binary as real root from the rootless user namesapce.
> @giuseppe Do you know if this is possible?

the setuid binary won't work in the user namespace since the root user is not mapped it, and likely nfs has some checks to prevent it being used from a user namespace anyway.

> So am I getting it right that the ONLY way to use rootless Podman with NFS4 for volume storage is to set the volume_path to a destination that is an already mounted NFS share?

yes, I think the only way to get it working at the moment is to mount nfs before entering the podman user namespace.

Ended up ditching NFS since after pre-mounting the share, podman fails with
being unable to set proper SELinux contexts. While NFSv4.2 technically
”supports” selinux, it doesn’t seem to support it anywhere near in full
(even with the appropriate flags set on the nfs server to enablr selinux
support).

Replaced NFSv4 with iSCSI and my podman is happy now. The idea of either
dropping rootless or selinux was a no-go as I consider podmans
compatibility with both THE reason to use podman in the first place.
